modern_echoes: Malaria maps are always partly maps of vectors, prophylaxis, and health-system reach rather than just case counts.
The contemporary question is often reintroduction or shifting suitability, not simply ancient endemic burden.
atlas.name: Malaria

origin_claim.date_or_era: Ancient human-parasite history with later species-specific dispersal
origin_claim.label: Deep African and Old World malaria ecologies
origin_claim.narrative: The broadest consensus places the deepest burden and evolutionary importance of falciparum malaria in Africa, while the wider human malaria story includes multiple Plasmodium species with different dispersal histories.

spread_routes.narrative: Enslavement, shipping, and mosquito-suitable plantation ecologies helped entrench malaria across the Atlantic world.
Malaria repeatedly advanced or retreated with irrigation, settlement, transport, and temperature, making altitude and land-use change central to its map.

spread_routes.from_label: Endemic African and Asian transmission zones
spread_routes.narrative: Modern malaria news often turns on imported cases, airport malaria, or local receptivity where competent Anopheles mosquitoes still exist.
spread_routes.to_label: Europe and North America reintroduction risk

atlas.subtitle: Anopheles ecologies, empire, labor, and the long retreat and return of a mosquito-borne world disease
atlas.summary: Malaria belongs in the atlas because it shows how vector ecology, land use, labor systems, altitude, war, and climate can redraw disease geography across centuries without one neat origin story doing all the work.
atlas.why_it_matters: It keeps the atlas from becoming virus-only and forces a map grammar built around mosquitoes, species differences, and ecological receptivity.

modern_echoes: A TB map is usually a map of crowding, incarceration, poverty, migration screening, and treatment failure rather than a map of dramatic emergence.
MDR and XDR TB turn ordinary travel and migration corridors into policy flashpoints.
atlas.name: Tuberculosis

origin_claim.date_or_era: Deep premodern origin with Neolithic and later human dispersal
origin_claim.label: African human-associated origin with later Eurasian and global coexpansion
origin_claim.narrative: Modern genomic work supports an African origin for the MTBC followed by dispersal with human populations, but the later epidemiologic story is built in cities, mines, barracks, prisons, and migration corridors.

spread_routes.narrative: Tuberculosis did not need empire to spread the first time; it rode with human movement and settlement long before modern surveillance existed.
The white-plague map is a crowding map: factories, tenements, prisons, workhouses, and other poorly ventilated spaces turned tuberculosis into a signature disease of industrial inequality.
spread_routes.to_label: Industrial European and North American cities

spread_routes.narrative: Modern tuberculosis geography is shaped by migration, HIV, drug resistance, and whether receiving health systems can diagnose and sustain treatment.
spread_routes.to_label: Europe and North America migration and screening corridors

atlas.subtitle: Human coexpansion, crowding, industry, migration, and the stubborn durability of airborne inequality
atlas.summary: Tuberculosis belongs in the atlas because it is both ancient and modern at once: a human co-traveler that thrives in crowding, poverty, institutional confinement, and uneven public health capacity.
atlas.why_it_matters: It turns the atlas toward chronic structural transmission instead of one spectacular frontier jump.

modern_echoes: Plague still matters because reservoir ecology in places like Madagascar and the western United States can still produce real outbreaks.
The modern lesson is less apocalypse than the durability of zoonotic reservoirs and infrastructural vulnerability.
atlas.name: Plague

origin_claim.date_or_era: Deep zoonotic reservoir history with repeated later human pandemic spillovers
origin_claim.label: Central Asian rodent-reservoir zone
origin_claim.narrative: The strongest broad frame places plague in Central Asian rodent reservoirs, with later eruptions into human trade and military networks rather than one single human-origin event.

spread_routes.from_label: Central Asian reservoir and caravan zones
spread_routes.narrative: Plague moved westward through overland and maritime trade interfaces before exploding into Mediterranean port cities.
spread_routes.to_label: Crimea and Black Sea trade ports

spread_routes.narrative: The Black Death route remains the most famous plague map: port to port, ship to city, then inland through dense human settlement.

spread_routes.date_or_era: Late nineteenth to early twentieth century third pandemic
spread_routes.narrative: The third pandemic made plague legible as a modern port disease moving through rats, ships, and urban infrastructure.
spread_routes.to_label: Bombay, San Francisco, Sydney, and other global ports

atlas.subtitle: Steppe reservoirs, siege and shipping, and the repeated collision of rodents, fleas, and empire
atlas.summary: Plague belongs in the atlas because it is one of the clearest historical demonstrations that reservoir ecology, trade corridors, warfare, and maritime ports can repeatedly rewire a continent.
atlas.why_it_matters: It lets the atlas bridge deep historical fear, genomic reconstruction, and the very practical map logic of rodents, fleas, and ports.

reference.diagnostics: PCR with strict biosafety and rapid isolation protocols.

latest_outbreak.label: 2026 Bundibugyo Ebola outbreak
latest_outbreak.location: Ituri Province, DRC, with imported cases reported in Kampala, Uganda
latest_outbreak.summary: Africa CDC declared the ongoing Bundibugyo ebolavirus disease outbreak affecting DRC and Uganda a Public Health Emergency of Continental Security on 18 May 2026, reporting about 395 suspected cases and 106 associated deaths in DRC and two cases with one death in Kampala, Uganda.

metrics_that_matter: Suspected, probable, confirmed, and ruled-out cases by health zone and report date.
Deaths inside and outside formal care, especially community deaths and healthcare-worker deaths.
Contact tracing performance: listed contacts, high-risk contacts, follow-up completeness, symptomatic contacts, and contacts lost to follow-up.
Laboratory pipeline: samples collected, tested locally, sent to reference labs, indeterminate results, and turnaround time.
Cross-border and urban signals: imported cases, airport or border screening, capital-city care, and whether a place is a transmission site or a detection/care site.
Countermeasure reality: PPE, isolation beds, IPC support, safe burial teams, vaccine candidates, therapeutics, and operational research timelines.
reference.name: Ebola virus disease
notable_outbreaks: Ebola was first identified in 1976 in what is now the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
The 2007 Bundibugyo outbreak in Uganda established Bundibugyo virus as a distinct Ebola-causing species.
West Africa 2014-2016 remains the defining modern Ebola catastrophe.
The 2018-2020 eastern DRC Ebola outbreak showed how insecurity, mobility, healthcare transmission, and community trust can make control brutally hard even with modern countermeasures.
reference.pathogen: Ebola viruses, including Bundibugyo virus in the current DRC/Uganda outbreak
reference.prevention: Isolation, PPE, safe burials, contact tracing, and vaccination where available.

related_stories.latest_update_summary: AJMC now foregrounds vaccination or vaccine policy in the story.

reference.research_caveats: Early Ebola counts are often an accounting artifact as much as an epidemic curve. When access, insecurity, transport, and laboratory confirmation lag, suspected cases and deaths can be a better warning signal than confirmed cases alone.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Fruit bats are the leading reservoir hypothesis; human amplification is contact-driven.
reference.severity: High fatality potential and intense healthcare-system stress.
reference.surveillance_note: Track the gap between suspected deaths and confirmed cases, not just the latest confirmed total. For fast-moving Ebola, testing delays, unsafe burials, health-worker infections, and contact follow-up failures are outbreak intelligence.
symptoms: Fever, weakness, gastrointestinal symptoms, and severe malaise.
Bleeding is not universal but remains part of the classic frame.
Rapid systemic deterioration can occur.
reference.transmission: Direct contact with blood, body fluids or contaminated materials
reference.treatment: Supportive care is central. Species-specific countermeasures matter: approved Ebola Zaire tools should not be lazily assumed to solve a Bundibugyo outbreak.
reference.vaccine_status: Vaccination can be decisive for some Ebola species and response settings, but Bundibugyo-specific vaccine and therapeutic availability remains a central uncertainty in the 2026 outbreak.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often defaults to cinematic fatality framing or distant-risk reassurance and misses the operational details that determine control: isolation delays, healthcare spread, burial practices, contact follow-up, laboratory turnaround, and whether the headline count is lagging the epidemic.
reference.why_reporters_care: Ebola remains a defining outbreak-desk disease because healthcare transmission, funeral practices, laboratory capacity, community trust, and international alarm can all move faster than the confirmed count.

reference.atlas_summary: Hantaviruses belong in the atlas because they break the habit of pretending every pathogen has one clean human-travel route. The real map is rodent-host biogeography, ecological exposure settings, and the split between Old World hemorrhagic-fever viruses and New World cardiopulmonary viruses.
reference.diagnostics: PCR, serology, and exposure history are central; timing matters.

latest_outbreak.label: Cruise-ship linked cluster
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO reported seven cases, including two laboratory-confirmed infections, three deaths, one critically ill patient and three mild suspected cases linked to cruise-ship travel.

metrics_that_matter: Confirmed versus suspected cases, because panic often outruns laboratory certainty.
Whether exposure points to rodents, enclosed lodging, or close-contact transmission.
Severity markers such as ICU care, pulmonary edema, shock, and deaths.
reference.name: Hantavirus syndrome
notable_outbreaks: Andes virus outbreaks in Argentina and Chile remain the canonical example of limited person-to-person hantavirus transmission.
reference.pathogen: Hantaviruses, including Andes virus in the Americas
reference.prevention: Rodent control, avoiding aerosolized rodent excreta, and close-contact precautions for suspected Andes virus clusters.

related_stories.latest_update_summary: New publisher/source coverage joined this story cluster: Reuters, USA Today.

reference.research_caveats: Small case counts and heterogeneous hantavirus species mean early claims about transmission mode can be unstable.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Wild rodents; Andes virus is especially associated with sigmodontine rodents in South America.
reference.severity: Potentially high fatality once cardiopulmonary syndrome develops.
reference.surveillance_note: Useful for rare severe respiratory clusters with rodent ecology or unusual travel-linked spread.
symptoms: Fever and severe myalgias after a prodromal phase.
Rapid progression to cough, pulmonary edema, and cardiopulmonary compromise in severe cases.
Thrombocytopenia and shock can appear in the more fulminant presentations.
reference.transmission: Rodent exposure; limited person-to-person transmission has been documented for Andes virus
reference.treatment: Supportive critical care, oxygenation, and hemodynamic support.
reference.vaccine_status: No licensed routine human vaccine is in general public-health use.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often treats all hantaviruses as one epidemiologic problem, when the key distinction is whether the event looks like classic rodent exposure or the much rarer Andes-virus pattern with limited human-to-human spread.
reference.why_reporters_care: This is a rare but frightening severe-disease story where a single unusual cluster can force questions about travel safety, rodent exposure, and whether Andes-virus-style person-to-person spread is in play.

reference.atlas_summary: Measles is a useful atlas pathogen because its deeper origin story is partly reconstructed from molecular-clock work, while its colonial spread story is brutally visible in the Americas and Pacific.
reference.diagnostics: PCR and serology, paired with travel and exposure tracing.
reference.incubation: Usually 7 to 14 days from exposure to rash onset.

latest_outbreak.label: United States resurgence
latest_outbreak.location: United States, including major Texas-New Mexico spread
latest_outbreak.summary: CDC's national measles summary reported 2,288 confirmed U.S. measles cases in 2025, with most cases outbreak-associated and many linked to outbreaks that crossed jurisdictions.

metrics_that_matter: Confirmed cases and outbreak-associated cases rather than rumor-level totals.
Vaccination status of cases and affected communities.
School, childcare, household, and travel links that show whether containment is failing.
reference.name: Measles
notable_outbreaks: 2019 Democratic Republic of the Congo epidemic.
2019 Samoa outbreak.
reference.prevention: Two-dose measles vaccination and rapid outbreak control around exposed contacts.

related_stories.latest_update_summary: New publisher/source coverage joined this story cluster: News On AIR, Prothom Alo English, Yemen Online.

reference.research_caveats: Administrative case totals and media tallies can lag or mix confirmed and probable cases across jurisdictions.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Humans are the only reservoir.
reference.severity: Can lead to pneumonia, encephalitis, and death, especially in under-immunized populations.
reference.surveillance_note: This is the disease to watch when vaccination gaps, school clusters, or travel-linked introductions begin to stack.
symptoms: Fever, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis.
Koplik spots can appear before rash.
A descending maculopapular rash follows the prodrome.
reference.treatment: Supportive care; vitamin A is relevant in specific clinical settings.
reference.vaccine_status: Highly effective routine vaccination exists; most outbreak stories are really immunity-gap stories.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Reporters often overfocus on raw case counts and underplay outbreak size, exposure settings, and vaccination status, which are usually the more informative signals.
reference.why_reporters_care: Measles is a clean reporter desk disease because it reveals vaccination gaps, school and household spread, travel-linked importation, and public-health capacity all at once.

reference.diagnostics: Culture, PCR, and exposure history are key.
reference.incubation: Usually a few days, but depends on exposure route.

latest_outbreak.label: Cattle-linked cutaneous anthrax cluster
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO reported confirmed human anthrax cases linked to cattle slaughter in Thailand, including at least one death and multiple hospitalized cutaneous anthrax cases.

metrics_that_matter: Route of exposure, because cutaneous and inhalational stories are not equivalent.
Animal deaths, slaughter events, and occupational links.
Whether antibiotics, antitoxin, and veterinary control arrived quickly enough.
reference.name: Anthrax
notable_outbreaks: This was the first reported anthrax death in Thailand since 1994, according to WHO.
reference.prevention: Livestock control, safe animal handling, and occupational protection.
reference.research_caveats: Early reports may not separate suspected cutaneous lesions from confirmed systemic disease.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Spores persist in soil and livestock environments; infected animals drive many human exposures.
reference.severity: Route-dependent, ranging from treatable cutaneous disease to life-threatening systemic infection.
reference.surveillance_note: Anthrax belongs in the desk because agricultural exposure clusters are often deeply local and easy to miss.
symptoms: Cutaneous anthrax causes a characteristic black eschar after papule and vesicle stages.
Inhalational and gastrointestinal disease are much more severe.
Exposure context matters heavily for clinical suspicion.
reference.transmission: Contact with infected animals or contaminated animal products
reference.treatment: Prompt antibiotics and supportive care; severe cases may need antitoxin strategies.
reference.vaccine_status: Human vaccination is not the standard public-health frame for most natural outbreaks; animal control and rapid treatment matter more.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often jumps to bioterror associations and misses that most real-world human anthrax stories are agricultural, occupational, and deeply local.
reference.why_reporters_care: Anthrax is a strong local accountability story because livestock practices, slaughter exposure, and rural reporting gaps can hide serious outbreaks in plain sight.

reference.atlas_summary: H5N1 belongs in the atlas because its contemporary geography is not a human epidemic map first; it is a bird-route, farm-system, and spillover map whose human layer remains contingent.
reference.diagnostics: Influenza PCR with subtype characterization and exposure history are critical.
reference.incubation: Usually a few days after exposure, though exact timing varies with exposure intensity.

latest_outbreak.label: Multistate U.S. dairy and poultry outbreak
latest_outbreak.summary: CDC described an ongoing multistate H5N1 outbreak in dairy cows, poultry and other animals, with 70 confirmed human infections in the United States since April 2024 and no identified person-to-person spread.

metrics_that_matter: Human case count and whether cases are mild, severe, or fatal.
Exposure setting, especially dairy, poultry, culling work, and wild-bird interfaces.
Any sign of household, healthcare, or unexplained transmission beyond direct animal exposure.
reference.name: Avian influenza A(H5N1)
notable_outbreaks: The current U.S. dairy-cattle episode changed the global H5N1 surveillance frame by moving the story deeper into mammalian agriculture.
reference.prevention: PPE for exposed workers, farm biosecurity, and aggressive animal surveillance.

related_stories.latest_update_summary: New publisher/source coverage joined this story cluster: Australian Broadcasting Corporation.

reference.research_caveats: Risk estimates change fast because surveillance intensity varies by farm system, species, and jurisdiction.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Wild birds, poultry, and now mammalian agricultural systems are central reservoirs and interfaces.
reference.severity: Severity ranges from mild conjunctivitis to severe respiratory disease with pandemic concern.
reference.surveillance_note: A top-tier reporter desk disease because animal, occupational, food-system and pandemic-risk lines all converge here.
symptoms: Fever and respiratory illness.
Conjunctivitis has been a recurring feature in some U.S. human cases.
Severe pneumonia can develop in more serious infections.
reference.transmission: Animal-to-human exposure; no sustained person-to-person spread identified in the cited U.S. outbreak
reference.treatment: Antivirals such as oseltamivir remain important when indicated, alongside supportive care.
reference.vaccine_status: Human seasonal flu vaccines do not solve this problem; candidate H5 vaccines and animal-control measures matter more.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often jumps straight to pandemic rhetoric without separating animal spillover, isolated human infection, and evidence of sustained person-to-person transmission.
reference.why_reporters_care: H5N1 is one of the few diseases where occupational exposure, food systems, animal surveillance, and pandemic-risk communications all converge in the same file.

reference.diagnostics: PCR early and serology later in the course.

latest_outbreak.label: 2025 global resurgence
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO reported 445,271 suspected and confirmed chikungunya cases and 155 deaths globally between 1 January and 30 September 2025, with outbreaks and resurgence across 40 countries.

metrics_that_matter: Outbreak size and geographic spread.
Vector range and travel-linked seeding into new places.
Persistent disability burden, not just deaths.
reference.name: Chikungunya
notable_outbreaks: The 2005-2006 La Reunion epidemic remains one of the signature modern chikungunya reference points.
reference.prevention: Vector control and bite avoidance remain central.
reference.research_caveats: Many surveillance systems blur suspected and confirmed cases, and long-term symptom burden is often undercounted.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Aedes mosquitoes are central to transmission.
reference.severity: Mortality is usually low, but disability burden can be substantial.
reference.surveillance_note: A good desk will watch chikungunya alongside dengue because the vectors, differential and public-health burden overlap.
symptoms: Abrupt fever.
Severe polyarthralgia or arthritis-like pain.
Rash and prolonged joint symptoms can follow.
reference.treatment: Supportive care with pain control.
reference.vaccine_status: No broadly used routine control vaccine anchors current outbreak response; vector control and public messaging remain central.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: It is often dismissed as a dengue-like illness, when the chronic joint-pain burden and workforce impact are a big part of the story.
reference.why_reporters_care: Chikungunya is a high-quality reporter disease because explosive outbreaks can be large, visually obvious, and politically disruptive even when mortality stays low.

reference.atlas_summary: Cholera is the classic map disease for showing how river basins, pilgrimage, military movement, and commercial shipping can convert a local ecology into repeated global pandemic waves.
reference.diagnostics: Clinical recognition in outbreak settings plus stool culture or rapid tests where available.

latest_outbreak.label: Ongoing global cholera upsurge
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO's epidemiological update #36 reported 18,715 new cholera and acute watery diarrhoea cases and 269 deaths in February 2026 across 17 countries, with the highest burden in the African Region.

metrics_that_matter: Cases, deaths, and case fatality ratio.
Water-system failure, displacement, flooding, or conflict context.
Treatment-center access and whether oral cholera vaccine campaigns are reaching the right places.
reference.name: Cholera
notable_outbreaks: The current WHO framing treats cholera as a prolonged global upsurge rather than a single isolated event.
reference.prevention: Safe water, sanitation, hygiene, and oral cholera vaccine in appropriate campaigns.
reference.research_caveats: Counts are often underestimates, and suspected-case definitions can vary sharply across humanitarian settings.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Aquatic environments and contaminated water systems are the main ecological frame.
reference.severity: High mortality is preventable, but only if rehydration is rapid and accessible.
reference.surveillance_note: One of the clearest diseases where fragile infrastructure, displacement and mortality can move fast enough to outrun media attention.
symptoms: Profuse watery diarrhea.
Vomiting and rapid dehydration.
Shock can develop quickly without treatment.
reference.treatment: Aggressive oral or IV rehydration; antibiotics in selected cases.
reference.vaccine_status: Oral cholera vaccines are important tools, but most reporting value still comes from infrastructure, access, and mortality context.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Media often reduce cholera to a simple contamination story and miss the fact that treatment access and infrastructure collapse are what turn cases into mass death.
reference.why_reporters_care: Cholera is one of the clearest diseases for showing how water, war, displacement, climate shocks, and state capacity become mortality.

reference.atlas_summary: Dengue makes the atlas useful for modern readers because it shows how vector range expansion, used-tire shipping, urbanization, and travel can redraw the map without requiring a single ancient origin story.
reference.diagnostics: PCR or antigen testing early, serology later, plus attention to exposure geography.
reference.incubation: Usually 4 to 10 days after an infectious mosquito bite.

latest_outbreak.label: Unprecedented global 2024 surge
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO's 2024 update recorded 14,434,584 dengue cases worldwide, including 11,201 deaths, with more than 90% of reported cases coming from the Region of the Americas.

metrics_that_matter: Cases, severe dengue, hospitalizations, and deaths.
Serotype shifts, if reported, because they can change severity expectations.
Urban mosquito control failure and the pace of geographic expansion.
reference.name: Dengue
notable_outbreaks: Brazil alone reported more than 10 million cases in the cited WHO 2024 update.
reference.prevention: Mosquito control, bite avoidance, and vaccine policy where appropriate.

related_stories.latest_update_summary: New publisher/source coverage joined this story cluster: ReliefWeb.

reference.research_caveats: Reported case totals can surge when testing changes, and severity comparisons across countries are often not apples to apples.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus mosquitoes drive transmission.
reference.severity: Most illness is self-limited, but severe dengue can decompensate quickly.
reference.surveillance_note: This belongs near the top of any global outbreak desk because climate, vector range and urban vulnerability all keep widening the playing field.
symptoms: Fever, headache, retro-orbital pain, and myalgias.
Rash and leukopenia are common.
A subset progress to severe dengue with plasma leakage or bleeding.
reference.treatment: Supportive care with careful fluid management.
reference.vaccine_status: Vaccines exist in a limited and policy-contingent way; most outbreak control still depends on vector ecology and clinical recognition.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage frequently treats dengue as a generic tropical fever and underplays serotype history, hospitalization pressure, and the difference between routine seasonal burden and exceptional surge.
reference.why_reporters_care: Dengue is now one of the best climate-and-city disease stories on earth because mosquito range, urban crowding, and health-system stress all show up in it.

reference.diagnostics: Clinical suspicion plus culture or PCR and toxin-focused confirmation when available.

latest_outbreak.label: African Region multi-country outbreak
latest_outbreak.location: Eight WHO African Region member states
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO reported 20,412 suspected diphtheria cases and 1,252 deaths across eight African countries in 2025, with Nigeria carrying the largest share of the burden.

metrics_that_matter: Suspected and confirmed cases, deaths, and age groups.
Antitoxin availability and outbreak-control capacity.
Vaccination coverage and whether the outbreak is concentrated in one displaced or under-served population.
reference.name: Diphtheria
notable_outbreaks: The scale and prolonged under-immunized burden make this more than a one-country story.
reference.prevention: Routine vaccination and rapid outbreak control around cases and carriers.
reference.research_caveats: Case definitions and laboratory confirmation can lag badly in large, stressed outbreaks.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Humans are the reservoir.
reference.severity: Can be fatal without antitoxin, antibiotics, and strong clinical support.
reference.surveillance_note: Diphtheria should function as a vaccination-systems alarm bell in this product.
symptoms: Sore throat, fever, and cervical swelling.
A gray pseudomembrane is the classic severe respiratory sign.
Toxin-mediated cardiac and neurologic complications drive much of the danger.
reference.treatment: Antitoxin, antibiotics, and airway management when needed.
reference.vaccine_status: Routine vaccination works, so major diphtheria stories usually reflect immunization failure, conflict, or system breakdown.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often treats it as an antique disease reappearing out of nowhere rather than as a predictable sign of low coverage and disrupted health systems.
reference.why_reporters_care: Diphtheria is a strong communications disease because it turns abstract vaccination decline into immediately legible airway and mortality risk.

reference.diagnostics: Serologic confirmation with IgM plus outbreak tracing.

latest_outbreak.label: Recurrent foodborne and community outbreaks
latest_outbreak.summary: Modern hepatitis A reporting repeatedly centers on foodborne clusters and prolonged community transmission in settings with sanitation stress or low vaccination coverage.

metrics_that_matter: Confirmed cases, hospitalizations, and deaths.
Foodborne versus community transmission setting.
Vaccination and post-exposure response among high-risk contacts.
reference.name: Hepatitis A
notable_outbreaks: Large U.S. person-to-person outbreaks in the late 2010s remain an important recent reference point.
reference.prevention: Vaccination, sanitation, food-safety control, and post-exposure prophylaxis where indicated.
reference.research_caveats: Long incubation makes exposure reconstruction difficult, so early source attribution can be shaky.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Humans are the reservoir; sanitation and food handling drive much of the outbreak frame.
reference.severity: Often self-limited but can be severe, especially in older adults or those with liver disease.
reference.surveillance_note: Hepatitis A is a good example of a disease that toggles between local sanitation story, foodborne recall story, and vaccination story.
symptoms: Fever, malaise, nausea, abdominal discomfort, and jaundice.
Children can have milder or asymptomatic infection.
Large foodborne and community outbreaks remain classic scenarios.
reference.treatment: Supportive care.
reference.vaccine_status: Vaccination is effective, but outbreak stories often hinge on whether local policy actually reached at-risk adults and unstable living settings.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often assumes every hepatitis A event is a restaurant or food-recall story, when prolonged person-to-person transmission can be the bigger issue.
reference.why_reporters_care: Hepatitis A is useful because it can move between foodborne recall, encampment, homelessness, travel, and sanitation stories without changing pathogen.

reference.diagnostics: Urinary antigen testing, PCR, culture, and environmental investigation.

latest_outbreak.label: Building and facility clusters
latest_outbreak.summary: Legionnaires' disease remains a recurring built-environment outbreak problem, with cases often linked to cooling towers, healthcare facilities, hotels, and complex water systems rather than a single modern global event.

metrics_that_matter: Cases, deaths, and affected facility types.
Cooling-tower, plumbing, or hospital-system findings.
Whether the cluster is ongoing or tied to a remediated source.
reference.name: Legionnaires' disease
notable_outbreaks: Facility-linked outbreaks are often underappreciated until environmental investigation catches up.
reference.prevention: Water-system maintenance, cooling-tower control, and environmental remediation.
reference.research_caveats: Outbreak attribution can change as environmental testing catches up, so early blame assignment is often noisy.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Built water systems, cooling towers, plumbing, and aerosol-generating water infrastructure.
reference.severity: Can be severe, particularly in older or medically vulnerable patients.
reference.surveillance_note: This is a strong reporter-desk disease because it sits at the intersection of environmental systems, hospitals, and local accountability.
symptoms: Pneumonia with fever, cough, and shortness of breath.
GI symptoms and confusion can be part of the picture.
Exposure clusters often emerge around facilities or buildings.
reference.transmission: Aerosolized contaminated water; not usually person-to-person
reference.treatment: Appropriate antibiotics plus supportive care.
reference.vaccine_status: No vaccine; the control story is environmental accountability and water-system management.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often centers only on a building scare and misses the bigger environmental-investigation and public-accountability story.
reference.why_reporters_care: Legionnaires' is a very good local-news and accountability disease because buildings, hospitals, hotels, and cooling towers turn infrastructure failure into human pneumonia clusters.

reference.atlas_summary: Malaria belongs in the atlas because it shows how vector ecology, land use, labor systems, altitude, war, and climate can redraw disease geography across centuries without one neat origin story doing all the work.
reference.diagnostics: Rapid diagnostic tests and microscopy remain the field core, with PCR for selected contexts.
reference.incubation: Often 7 to 30 days, depending on species and immunity.

latest_outbreak.label: Persistent high-burden global transmission
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO's recent malaria reporting continues to frame malaria less as a single outbreak than as a persistent high-burden transmission crisis shaped by insecticide resistance, climate variability, and health-system fragility.

metrics_that_matter: Cases, deaths, and severe disease burden in children.
Rainfall, displacement, stock-outs, and vector-control breakdown.
Drug and insecticide resistance signals.
reference.name: Malaria
notable_outbreaks: For a desk product, seasonal or conflict-linked malaria spikes matter even when they are under-covered as news.
reference.pathogen: Plasmodium parasites, especially Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax
reference.prevention: Bed nets, vector control, chemoprevention, seasonal prevention strategies, and vaccination in some settings.
reference.research_caveats: Routine surveillance often underestimates burden, and comparisons across countries depend heavily on testing access and reporting completeness.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Anopheles mosquitoes are the transmission vector; humans are the main reservoir in most settings.
reference.severity: A chronic global emergency with severe disease and death concentrated in young children and vulnerable populations.
reference.surveillance_note: Malaria deserves broader retrieval because a lot of important rural transmission news never gets framed as a headline outbreak.
symptoms: Fever, chills, headache, and malaise.
Severe malaria can involve anemia, cerebral disease, acidosis, or organ failure.
Travel history and exposure ecology remain fundamental to suspicion.
reference.treatment: Artemisinin-based combination therapy for uncomplicated malaria; IV artesunate for severe disease.
reference.vaccine_status: Vaccination is now part of the toolkit in some settings, but nets, vector control, and treatment access still dominate the burden story.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often waits for a spectacular outbreak frame and misses the fact that routine seasonal or conflict-linked surges are already major epidemiologic events.
reference.why_reporters_care: Malaria is a foundational global-health reporting file because it links endemic burden, climate, resistance, conflict, childhood mortality, and rural neglect.

reference.diagnostics: PCR and strict containment-oriented laboratory workflows.

latest_outbreak.label: First recorded Ethiopian outbreak
latest_outbreak.location: South Ethiopia Region and Sidama Region, Ethiopia
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO reported 19 total Marburg cases, including 14 confirmed and 14 deaths overall when probable cases are included, in Ethiopia's first recognized Marburg outbreak.

metrics_that_matter: Confirmed cases and deaths.
Mine, cave, bat, household, or healthcare exposure context.
Any sign of spread beyond the initial district or healthcare setting.
reference.name: Marburg virus disease
notable_outbreaks: The first recognized Ethiopian outbreak widened the modern Marburg geography.
reference.prevention: Isolation, PPE, contact tracing, and reservoir-exposure awareness.
reference.research_caveats: Outbreaks are usually small, so it is easy to overinterpret noisy early severity estimates.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Rousettus fruit bats are the key reservoir signal in classic framing.
reference.severity: High fatality potential with major outbreak-control implications.
reference.surveillance_note: Any unexplained hemorrhagic fever cluster in East Africa now has a slightly wider differential than it did a few years ago.
symptoms: Abrupt fever and severe constitutional illness.
Gastrointestinal symptoms and hemorrhagic features can follow.
Severe multi-organ disease can develop quickly.
reference.transmission: Direct contact with body fluids, contaminated materials and infected animal reservoirs
reference.treatment: Supportive care; no routine curative therapy is the default public-health frame.
reference.vaccine_status: No routine widely deployed preventive vaccine anchors most Marburg responses.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Media often treat Marburg as interchangeable with Ebola, when geography, reservoir context, and outbreak scale still matter a great deal.
reference.why_reporters_care: Marburg matters because even small clusters can force rapid reassessment of differential diagnosis, hospital preparedness, and regional spillover risk.

reference.diagnostics: Blood culture, CSF evaluation, PCR, and public-health serogrouping are all important.
reference.incubation: Usually 3 to 4 days, with a broader range of about 2 to 10 days.

latest_outbreak.label: Expanding meningitis belt pressure
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO and regional reporting have continued to emphasize recurrent meningococcal meningitis pressure across meningitis-belt countries, where serogroup shifts and strained health systems can rapidly change outbreak control needs.

metrics_that_matter: Confirmed invasive cases and deaths.
Serogroup, because it changes vaccine and response implications.
Dormitory, campus, military, or meningitis-belt cluster context.
reference.name: Meningococcal disease
notable_outbreaks: Serogroup changes have repeatedly altered the control picture across the African meningitis belt.
reference.prevention: Vaccination, chemoprophylaxis for close contacts, and fast cluster investigation.
reference.research_caveats: Single severe cases attract huge attention, but outbreak significance depends on linkage, serogroup, and public-health confirmation.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Humans are the reservoir; transmission often intensifies in crowded settings.
reference.severity: High-stakes disease because fulminant sepsis and meningitis can progress quickly and kill rapidly.
reference.surveillance_note: This belongs on the desk because local clusters can become national panic stories very quickly.
symptoms: Abrupt fever, headache, and neck stiffness in meningitis presentations.
Petechial or purpuric rash can signal invasive bloodstream disease.
Clinical deterioration can be rapid enough to make same-day recognition decisive.
reference.treatment: Immediate empiric antibiotics and intensive supportive care.
reference.vaccine_status: Vaccination is central, but the real desk issue is which serogroup is circulating and how quickly control measures are moving.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often treats every case as a generic meningitis story and misses the importance of invasive disease, serogroup, and contact prophylaxis.
reference.why_reporters_care: Meningococcal disease is intensely newsworthy because it is fast, terrifying, often affects schools or young adults, and can force rapid public-health action.

reference.diagnostics: PCR and careful exposure or travel history remain central.

latest_outbreak.label: Global update with Saudi and travel-associated French cases
latest_outbreak.location: Saudi Arabia and travel-associated cases in France
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO reported 19 MERS cases and four deaths globally in 2025 through 21 December, including healthcare-linked transmission in Saudi Arabia and imported cases in France.

metrics_that_matter: Healthcare-linked cases and deaths.
Travel-associated exportation beyond the Arabian Peninsula.
Camel exposure versus healthcare exposure, because that changes the control story.
reference.name: Middle East respiratory syndrome (MERS)
notable_outbreaks: The 2015 Republic of Korea outbreak remains the classic reminder that travel-linked hospital amplification can globalize a regional virus.
reference.prevention: Infection control in hospitals and reducing risky camel-related exposure.
reference.research_caveats: Severe-case bias can distort apparent fatality and make mild transmission less visible.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Dromedary camels are the key animal reservoir in current public-health framing.
reference.severity: Can be severe, especially in older or medically vulnerable patients.
reference.surveillance_note: For newsroom use, the hospital-cluster angle is often more important than the raw case count.
symptoms: Fever, cough, and shortness of breath.
Pneumonia is common in more serious cases.
Healthcare-associated clusters can include milder and asymptomatic infections.
reference.transmission: Camel-to-human spillover and close-contact spread, especially in healthcare settings
reference.treatment: Supportive care.
reference.vaccine_status: No routine public vaccine is the core control tool; hospital infection control and camel-exposure context still dominate.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often treats MERS as a dead coronavirus relic, when the operational question is whether hospital amplification is recurring and whether imported cases change risk elsewhere.
reference.why_reporters_care: MERS remains a prototype hospital-cluster and travel-linked importation story where a modest case count can still matter a lot.

reference.atlas_summary: Mpox belongs in the atlas because it combines a geographically anchored Central/West African history with abrupt export through wildlife trade and then a very different twenty-first century global transmission pattern.
reference.diagnostics: Lesion-based PCR is the practical core diagnostic tool.
reference.incubation: Often about 1 to 2 weeks, but can vary.

latest_outbreak.label: Broader clade Ib transmission
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO reported broader transmission of clade Ib mpox, including locally acquired infections in multiple WHO regions and continued substantial outbreaks in African countries.

metrics_that_matter: Clade and whether cases are imported or locally acquired.
Household, healthcare, sexual-network, or congregate-setting transmission.
Severity, pediatric involvement, and evidence of sustained spread outside the initial network.
reference.name: Mpox
notable_outbreaks: The 2022 multi-country clade IIb outbreak remains the watershed event for modern global mpox reporting.
reference.pathogen: Monkeypox virus, including clade Ib and clade IIb
reference.prevention: Risk communication, contact precautions, vaccination in defined settings, and rapid case finding.
reference.research_caveats: Case ascertainment varies heavily with access to testing and with how stigmatized the exposed population is.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Animal reservoir remains incompletely defined; human-to-human transmission is central in current surveillance.
reference.severity: Usually self-limited but can be severe in vulnerable patients and in settings with delayed diagnosis.
reference.surveillance_note: The key distinction to preserve is clade, mode of spread, and whether cases are travel-linked or locally acquired.
symptoms: Fever, malaise, lymphadenopathy, and rash.
Painful mucosal or genital lesions may dominate some current outbreak presentations.
Rash burden and lesion distribution vary by clade and transmission network.
reference.transmission: Close physical contact, including sexual contact; household and healthcare transmission also occur
reference.treatment: Supportive care, infection control, and antivirals in selected higher-risk cases.
reference.vaccine_status: Vaccination exists but use is targeted, supply and strategy depend on context, and clade-specific framing still matters.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: The central mistake is flattening clade, route of spread, and affected networks into one undifferentiated global mpox narrative.
reference.why_reporters_care: Mpox keeps generating news because social transmission networks, travel, stigma, clade differences, and global inequity collide in a single outbreak story.

reference.diagnostics: PCR and high-containment laboratory workflows are central.
reference.incubation: Often 4 to 14 days, though longer intervals have been reported.

latest_outbreak.label: Kerala cluster
latest_outbreak.summary: Recent South Asian Nipah reporting has continued to center on Kerala, where public-health responses have emphasized rapid contact tracing, hospital precautions, and fruit-bat spillover concern.

metrics_that_matter: Hospital-linked and household-linked transmission.
Respiratory involvement versus mainly encephalitic presentation.
Case fatality and whether contact tracing is staying ahead of the cluster.
reference.name: Nipah virus disease
notable_outbreaks: Bangladesh and India outbreaks remain the core modern human-to-human Nipah reference points.
reference.prevention: Avoiding bat-contaminated food exposures, infection control, and rapid contact tracing.
reference.research_caveats: Nipah outbreaks are often small and intensely scrutinized, which makes early severity and transmission claims prone to swing.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Fruit bats are the key reservoir in the standard public-health frame.
reference.severity: High fatality potential and intense concern for hospital and household amplification.
reference.surveillance_note: Nipah deserves special attention because respiratory and encephalitic framing can shift fast, and hospital spread matters.
symptoms: Fever, headache, and rapidly progressive encephalitic illness.
Respiratory symptoms can also be prominent.
Neurologic deterioration is a major red-flag feature.
reference.transmission: Bat spillover, contaminated food exposure, and person-to-person spread in close-contact settings
reference.treatment: Supportive care.
reference.vaccine_status: No routine vaccine is currently the practical outbreak tool; surveillance and infection control are doing the heavy lifting.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often leans into apocalypse language without separating bat spillover, contaminated food exposure, and the much more consequential question of documented person-to-person spread.
reference.why_reporters_care: Nipah is one of the few pathogens where even a small cluster can raise immediate questions about encephalitis, respiratory spread, hospital amplification, and pandemic risk.

reference.diagnostics: PCR panels, outbreak epidemiology, and facility investigation.

latest_outbreak.label: Recurring cruise and institutional outbreaks
latest_outbreak.summary: Norovirus remains one of the most recurrent outbreak categories for cruise ships, schools, and long-term-care facilities, with rapid attack rates and heavy local reporting but inconsistent national attention.

metrics_that_matter: Attack rate and facility type.
Cruise, school, long-term-care, or food-service setting.
Whether the source is environmental, person-to-person, or foodborne.
reference.name: Norovirus
notable_outbreaks: Cruise-ship outbreaks keep reminding editors that enormous audience interest can attach to pathogens that are usually medically self-limited.
reference.prevention: Hand hygiene, environmental cleaning, exclusion of ill food handlers, and rapid institution-level control.
reference.research_caveats: Local outbreaks are numerous and inconsistently reported, so media visibility can badly misrepresent actual burden.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Humans and contaminated environments are the practical outbreak reservoir frame.
reference.severity: Usually self-limited but highly disruptive, especially for institutions and travel settings.
reference.surveillance_note: This is one of the most reliable high-volume local outbreak categories and should be surfaced more aggressively.
symptoms: Abrupt vomiting and diarrhea.
Abdominal cramps and low-grade fever are common.
Explosive facility outbreaks are typical.
reference.transmission: Fecal-oral, foodborne, waterborne, and environmental contamination
reference.treatment: Supportive care and rehydration.
reference.vaccine_status: No routine population vaccine drives present-day outbreak control.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: It is often treated as trivial because mortality is low, but the real story is rapid attack rate, institutional disruption, and public confidence.
reference.why_reporters_care: Norovirus is highly useful for a desk because it produces the kind of explosive local outbreak that readers immediately understand, especially on cruise ships and in institutions.

reference.diagnostics: PCR and arboviral differential testing where available.

latest_outbreak.label: Americas expansion
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO reported 11,634 confirmed Oropouche cases and two deaths across ten countries and one territory as of 25 November 2024, with regional spread into places not previously reporting cases.

metrics_that_matter: Confirmed cases and newly affected countries or regions.
Whether clinicians are distinguishing it from dengue and other arboviruses.
Pregnancy, neurologic, or unusually severe presentations if they emerge.
reference.name: Oropouche virus disease
notable_outbreaks: The 2024 expansion mattered because it turned Oropouche from a niche arbovirus story into a continental surveillance story.
reference.prevention: Vector control and bite avoidance.
reference.research_caveats: Because testing is patchy, apparent expansion can reflect both true spread and better recognition.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Biting midges are a core transmission vector; mosquito involvement also matters in public-health framing.
reference.severity: Usually non-fatal but epidemiologically important because it can hide inside broader arboviral surveillance.
reference.surveillance_note: Worth keeping visible because it can hide inside dengue-like syndrome surveillance.
symptoms: Acute febrile illness with headache and myalgias.
Clinical presentation can overlap heavily with dengue-like syndromes.
Some patients experience recurrent symptoms.
reference.treatment: Supportive care.
reference.vaccine_status: No routine vaccine anchors public-health control; differential testing and vector surveillance matter more.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: It is often covered as an obscure tropical curiosity rather than as a surveillance-classification problem that can distort broader arbovirus reporting.
reference.why_reporters_care: Oropouche deserves attention because it can masquerade as dengue-like illness while signaling that arboviral ecology is shifting into new places.

reference.diagnostics: PCR, culture, and careful clinical timing.
reference.incubation: Usually 5 to 10 days, though it can be longer.

latest_outbreak.label: Post-pandemic resurgence pattern
latest_outbreak.summary: Recent pertussis reporting has reflected broader post-pandemic resurgence patterns, with public-health attention on school clusters, infant protection, and under-recognition in older age groups.

metrics_that_matter: Infant cases, hospitalizations, and deaths.
School or household cluster growth.
Maternal immunization, booster uptake, and timing of diagnosis.
reference.name: Pertussis
notable_outbreaks: Pertussis repeatedly reminds reporters that vaccine-preventable does not mean epidemiologically solved.
outbreak_settings: Communities with waning immunity or low uptake
reference.prevention: Vaccination, booster policy, and protecting infants through maternal immunization and rapid case recognition.
reference.research_caveats: Reported burden depends heavily on testing intensity and clinical suspicion, so many systems still undercount adult disease.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Humans are the reservoir.
reference.severity: Often prolonged and disruptive, with severe risk concentrated in infants.
reference.surveillance_note: Worth keeping on the desk because infant risk, school spread, and booster debates all make it newsy.
symptoms: Catarrhal illness early, followed by paroxysmal cough.
Classic whoop is not universal, especially in adults.
Infants face the greatest severe-disease risk.
reference.treatment: Macrolides and supportive care, with prophylaxis for defined contacts.
reference.vaccine_status: Vaccination exists, but waning immunity, booster policy, and infant protection remain live public-health issues.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often assumes a classic whooping presentation, when many adolescent and adult cases are clinically subtler but epidemiologically important.
reference.why_reporters_care: Pertussis repeatedly becomes news because it combines school spread, infant risk, vaccine debates, and underrecognized adult transmission.

reference.diagnostics: Stool testing, environmental surveillance, and genomic linkage are central.
reference.incubation: Often 7 to 21 days for paralytic disease, though infection may be silent.

latest_outbreak.label: cVDPV2 detection in healthy children and environment
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO classified the Papua New Guinea cVDPV2 detections as a polio outbreak after linked type 2 poliovirus was found in environmental samples and in stool specimens from two healthy children in Morobe province.

metrics_that_matter: Environmental detections and genomic linkage across sites.
Whether any acute flaccid paralysis cases have been confirmed.
Vaccination campaign scope, immunity gaps, and cross-border spread risk.
reference.name: Poliomyelitis / cVDPV2
notable_outbreaks: Papua New Guinea also experienced a cVDPV1 outbreak in 2018.
reference.pathogen: Poliovirus, especially circulating vaccine-derived poliovirus type 2 in the cited event
reference.prevention: Vaccination, sanitation, and aggressive outbreak response immunization.

related_stories.latest_update_summary: PubMed Infectious Disease Search now foregrounds vaccination or vaccine policy in the story.

reference.research_caveats: A single detection can matter a great deal, but risk interpretation depends on sequencing context and surveillance quality.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Humans are the reservoir; transmission is tightly linked to sanitation and immunity gaps.
reference.severity: The surveillance burden is driven by silent spread plus the catastrophic consequence of paralytic disease.
reference.surveillance_note: Environmental detections matter here even when paralytic disease is not yet evident.
symptoms: Most infections are asymptomatic.
Mild febrile or gastrointestinal illness can occur.
A small proportion progress to acute flaccid paralysis.
reference.treatment: Supportive care only.
reference.vaccine_status: Vaccination remains the decisive preventive tool, but the key reporting distinction is wild poliovirus versus vaccine-derived poliovirus.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often waits for paralysis, when the actual public-health story is usually the silent circulation signal that appears first in sewage or healthy children.
reference.why_reporters_care: Polio is a near-eradication story where environmental detections, asymptomatic carriage, and vaccination politics matter as much as paralytic disease.

reference.diagnostics: Exposure assessment is central; laboratory confirmation often comes late and may require specialized testing.
reference.incubation: Usually weeks to months, but highly variable.

latest_outbreak.label: Persistent rural dog-mediated burden
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO continues to frame rabies as a preventable but still deadly disease concentrated in underserved settings where dog vaccination and post-exposure prophylaxis access remain inadequate.

metrics_that_matter: Access to post-exposure prophylaxis and immunoglobulin.
Dog vaccination coverage and bite-source ecology.
Whether cases are clustered in rural underserved areas.
reference.name: Rabies
notable_outbreaks: For desk purposes, rabies matters because devastating local stories keep happening even when rich-country editors barely notice.
outbreak_settings: Areas with weak post-exposure prophylaxis access
reference.prevention: Dog vaccination, bite prevention, and rapid access to post-exposure prophylaxis.
reference.research_caveats: Human case counts are often severe underestimates because diagnostic confirmation is limited where burden is highest.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Dogs remain the main source of human rabies globally, though wildlife reservoirs matter regionally.
reference.severity: Essentially universally fatal once symptomatic, which is why exposure response is the real public-health battleground.
reference.surveillance_note: This is exactly the kind of severe rural infectious-disease burden that can disappear if the intake is too urban and too English-headline dependent.
symptoms: Early fever and paresthesia at the bite site can precede neurologic illness.
Hydrophobia, aerophobia, agitation, or paralysis mark advanced disease.
Once clinical disease begins, the prognosis is overwhelmingly poor.
reference.treatment: Post-exposure prophylaxis before symptoms; supportive care after disease onset.
reference.vaccine_status: Pre-exposure vaccination is selective, but the real population-level control tool is dog vaccination plus reliable access to post-exposure prophylaxis.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often fixates on the horror of symptomatic disease and underreports the policy issue that actually matters: whether bite victims can get prompt prophylaxis.
reference.why_reporters_care: Rabies remains one of the starkest diseases in communications because nearly every symptomatic human case is a preventable systems failure.

reference.diagnostics: PCR or serology interpreted with animal and vector context.

latest_outbreak.label: West African cross-border outbreak
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO reported 404 confirmed human Rift Valley fever cases and 42 deaths across Mauritania and Senegal, with continued concern about mosquito ecology and livestock movement.

metrics_that_matter: Human cases and deaths.
Animal illness, abortion storms, and livestock movement.
Flooding, mosquito surges, and cross-border spread.
reference.name: Rift Valley fever
notable_outbreaks: Cross-border livestock movement is one of the recurring reasons RVF repeatedly defeats narrow national framing.
reference.prevention: Mosquito control, livestock vaccination policy, and safer animal-handling practices.
reference.research_caveats: Human and animal surveillance rarely line up perfectly, so early burden estimates can understate the scale.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Mosquito vectors and infected livestock are both central.
reference.severity: Often mild in humans but capable of severe complications and major livestock disruption.
reference.surveillance_note: This is classic One Health territory: weather, vectors, herds and human illness all move together.
symptoms: Fever and flu-like illness in many cases.
Some patients develop ocular, hemorrhagic, or encephalitic complications.
Animal abortion storms are part of the wider outbreak signal.
reference.treatment: Supportive care.
reference.vaccine_status: Human vaccination is not the routine anchor; livestock policy and vector ecology drive most prevention decisions.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: It is often framed as just another mosquito story, when livestock abortion storms and animal-trade movement are usually central to the real outbreak picture.
reference.why_reporters_care: Rift Valley fever is one of the best One Health reporting files because rainfall, livestock, mosquitoes, and human disease all move together.

reference.atlas_summary: Yellow fever is one of the clearest examples of a pathogen whose historical geography was shaped by maritime movement, tropical mosquito ecology, port infrastructure, and imperial warfare.
reference.diagnostics: PCR and serology, interpreted with vaccination history and geography.

latest_outbreak.label: Americas resurgence
latest_outbreak.location: Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador and Peru
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO reported 212 confirmed yellow fever cases and 85 deaths in the Americas as of late April 2025, a threefold increase over 2024 totals.

metrics_that_matter: Confirmed cases and deaths, especially in newly affected geography.
Whether transmission remains sylvatic or shows signs of urban spread.
Vaccination coverage, campaign timing, and traveler risk messaging.
reference.name: Yellow fever
notable_outbreaks: The cited WHO update notes spread beyond the Amazon region into places such as Sao Paulo and Tolima.
reference.prevention: Vaccination remains the decisive preventive tool.
reference.research_caveats: Small outbreaks can still matter a lot, and cross-country comparisons are complicated by uneven surveillance and vaccination histories.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Mosquito cycles involving non-human primates and humans define the classic ecology.
reference.severity: Potentially high fatality in the toxic phase.
reference.surveillance_note: When sylvatic transmission escapes its expected geography, this deserves very fast escalation.
symptoms: Fever, headache, and myalgias in the early phase.
A toxic phase can bring jaundice, bleeding, and organ failure.
Not every case progresses to severe disease, but the severe end is dramatic.
reference.treatment: Supportive care only.
reference.vaccine_status: Vaccination is highly effective, so outbreaks are usually surveillance-and-coverage failures rather than biomedical mysteries.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: The common mistake is failing to distinguish forest-cycle spillover from the much scarier possibility of wider urban transmission.
reference.why_reporters_care: Yellow fever matters because a rise in sylvatic spillover, urban encroachment, or vaccine gaps can turn a seemingly remote arbovirus story into a major regional emergency.

reference.diagnostics: PCR and serology interpreted cautiously because of cross-reactive flavivirus issues.
reference.incubation: Usually a few days to about two weeks.

latest_outbreak.label: Brazil-centered Americas epidemic
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO's fact sheet still anchors the defining recent major Zika outbreak in the Brazil-led 2015 epidemic that triggered the 2016 PHEIC over microcephaly and congenital abnormalities.

metrics_that_matter: Transmission geography and whether local mosquito spread is occurring.
Pregnancy-related surveillance and congenital outcomes.
Travel guidance and vector-control failures.
reference.name: Zika virus disease
notable_outbreaks: WHO notes that transmission persists at low levels in several countries even after the peak epidemic passed.
reference.prevention: Mosquito control, bite avoidance, and pregnancy-focused travel and exposure guidance.
reference.research_caveats: Serology is messy because of flavivirus cross-reactivity, so burden estimates and exposure histories can be difficult to interpret cleanly.
reference.reservoir_or_vector: Aedes mosquitoes are central; congenital and sexual transmission matter for public-health framing.
reference.severity: Usually mild in the adult host but potentially severe in fetal and congenital outcomes.
reference.surveillance_note: For a reporter desk, congenital impact matters as much as raw incident case counts.
symptoms: Often mild or asymptomatic.
Fever, rash, arthralgia, and conjunctivitis are classic when symptomatic.
Pregnancy-related fetal risk is the defining severity concern.
reference.transmission: Aedes mosquitoes; vertical and sexual transmission also documented
reference.treatment: Supportive care.
reference.vaccine_status: No broadly deployed routine vaccine anchors present-day public-health response.
reference.what_reporters_get_wrong: Coverage often focuses only on the mild adult syndrome and underweights the pregnancy and congenital surveillance stakes.
reference.why_reporters_care: Zika matters because congenital outcomes, pregnancy guidance, travel messaging, and mosquito ecology can turn a mild-adult disease story into a major public-health communications event.

latest_outbreak.label: 2026 Bundibugyo Ebola outbreak
latest_outbreak.location: Ituri Province, DRC, with imported cases reported in Kampala, Uganda
latest_outbreak.summary: Africa CDC declared the ongoing Bundibugyo ebolavirus disease outbreak affecting DRC and Uganda a Public Health Emergency of Continental Security on 18 May 2026, reporting about 395 suspected cases and 106 associated deaths in DRC and two cases with one death in Kampala, Uganda.

metrics_that_matter: Suspected, probable, confirmed, and ruled-out cases by health zone and report date.
Deaths inside and outside formal care, especially community deaths and healthcare-worker deaths.
Contact tracing performance: listed contacts, high-risk contacts, follow-up completeness, symptomatic contacts, and contacts lost to follow-up.
Laboratory pipeline: samples collected, tested locally, sent to reference labs, indeterminate results, and turnaround time.
Cross-border and urban signals: imported cases, airport or border screening, capital-city care, and whether a place is a transmission site or a detection/care site.
Countermeasure reality: PPE, isolation beds, IPC support, safe burial teams, vaccine candidates, therapeutics, and operational research timelines.
related_references.name: Ebola virus disease
related_references.pathogen: Ebola viruses, including Bundibugyo virus in the current DRC/Uganda outbreak
related_references.research_caveats: Early Ebola counts are often an accounting artifact as much as an epidemic curve. When access, insecurity, transport, and laboratory confirmation lag, suspected cases and deaths can be a better warning signal than confirmed cases alone.
related_references.surveillance_note: Track the gap between suspected deaths and confirmed cases, not just the latest confirmed total. For fast-moving Ebola, testing delays, unsafe burials, health-worker infections, and contact follow-up failures are outbreak intelligence.
related_references.transmission: Direct contact with blood, body fluids or contaminated materials
related_references.treatment: Supportive care is central. Species-specific countermeasures matter: approved Ebola Zaire tools should not be lazily assumed to solve a Bundibugyo outbreak.
related_references.vaccine_status: Vaccination can be decisive for some Ebola species and response settings, but Bundibugyo-specific vaccine and therapeutic availability remains a central uncertainty in the 2026 outbreak.

latest_outbreak.label: Cruise-ship linked cluster
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO reported seven cases, including two laboratory-confirmed infections, three deaths, one critically ill patient and three mild suspected cases linked to cruise-ship travel.

metrics_that_matter: Confirmed versus suspected cases, because panic often outruns laboratory certainty.
Whether exposure points to rodents, enclosed lodging, or close-contact transmission.
Severity markers such as ICU care, pulmonary edema, shock, and deaths.
related_references.name: Hantavirus syndrome
related_references.pathogen: Hantaviruses, including Andes virus in the Americas
related_references.research_caveats: Small case counts and heterogeneous hantavirus species mean early claims about transmission mode can be unstable.
related_references.surveillance_note: Useful for rare severe respiratory clusters with rodent ecology or unusual travel-linked spread.
related_references.transmission: Rodent exposure; limited person-to-person transmission has been documented for Andes virus
related_references.treatment: Supportive critical care, oxygenation, and hemodynamic support.
related_references.vaccine_status: No licensed routine human vaccine is in general public-health use.

latest_outbreak.location: United States, including major Texas-New Mexico spread
latest_outbreak.summary: CDC's national measles summary reported 2,288 confirmed U.S. measles cases in 2025, with most cases outbreak-associated and many linked to outbreaks that crossed jurisdictions.

metrics_that_matter: Confirmed cases and outbreak-associated cases rather than rumor-level totals.

related_references.name: Measles
related_references.research_caveats: Administrative case totals and media tallies can lag or mix confirmed and probable cases across jurisdictions.
related_references.surveillance_note: This is the disease to watch when vaccination gaps, school clusters, or travel-linked introductions begin to stack.
related_references.treatment: Supportive care; vitamin A is relevant in specific clinical settings.
related_references.vaccine_status: Highly effective routine vaccination exists; most outbreak stories are really immunity-gap stories.

latest_outbreak.label: Multistate U.S. dairy and poultry outbreak
latest_outbreak.summary: CDC described an ongoing multistate H5N1 outbreak in dairy cows, poultry and other animals, with 70 confirmed human infections in the United States since April 2024 and no identified person-to-person spread.

metrics_that_matter: Human case count and whether cases are mild, severe, or fatal.
Exposure setting, especially dairy, poultry, culling work, and wild-bird interfaces.
Any sign of household, healthcare, or unexplained transmission beyond direct animal exposure.
related_references.name: Avian influenza A(H5N1)
related_references.research_caveats: Risk estimates change fast because surveillance intensity varies by farm system, species, and jurisdiction.
related_references.surveillance_note: A top-tier reporter desk disease because animal, occupational, food-system and pandemic-risk lines all converge here.
related_references.transmission: Animal-to-human exposure; no sustained person-to-person spread identified in the cited U.S. outbreak
related_references.treatment: Antivirals such as oseltamivir remain important when indicated, alongside supportive care.
related_references.vaccine_status: Human seasonal flu vaccines do not solve this problem; candidate H5 vaccines and animal-control measures matter more.

latest_outbreak.label: cVDPV2 detection in healthy children and environment
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO classified the Papua New Guinea cVDPV2 detections as a polio outbreak after linked type 2 poliovirus was found in environmental samples and in stool specimens from two healthy children in Morobe province.

metrics_that_matter: Environmental detections and genomic linkage across sites.
Whether any acute flaccid paralysis cases have been confirmed.
Vaccination campaign scope, immunity gaps, and cross-border spread risk.
related_references.name: Poliomyelitis / cVDPV2
related_references.pathogen: Poliovirus, especially circulating vaccine-derived poliovirus type 2 in the cited event
related_references.research_caveats: A single detection can matter a great deal, but risk interpretation depends on sequencing context and surveillance quality.
related_references.surveillance_note: Environmental detections matter here even when paralytic disease is not yet evident.
related_references.vaccine_status: Vaccination remains the decisive preventive tool, but the key reporting distinction is wild poliovirus versus vaccine-derived poliovirus.

latest_outbreak.label: Unprecedented global 2024 surge
latest_outbreak.summary: WHO's 2024 update recorded 14,434,584 dengue cases worldwide, including 11,201 deaths, with more than 90% of reported cases coming from the Region of the Americas.

metrics_that_matter: Cases, severe dengue, hospitalizations, and deaths.
Serotype shifts, if reported, because they can change severity expectations.
Urban mosquito control failure and the pace of geographic expansion.
related_references.name: Dengue
related_references.research_caveats: Reported case totals can surge when testing changes, and severity comparisons across countries are often not apples to apples.
related_references.surveillance_note: This belongs near the top of any global outbreak desk because climate, vector range and urban vulnerability all keep widening the playing field.
related_references.treatment: Supportive care with careful fluid management.
related_references.vaccine_status: Vaccines exist in a limited and policy-contingent way; most outbreak control still depends on vector ecology and clinical recognition.

story_items.summary: Three more children died with symptoms consistent with measles in 24 hours until 8:00 am today, Monday, bringing Bangladesh’s combined tally of confirmed and suspected measles-related deaths to 741. Since 15 March, a total of 89,734 patients with suspected measles have been hospitalised across the country, of whom 86,062 have recovered, according to DGHS data. With the latest update, the number of suspected measles deaths has risen to 648, while the number of laboratory-confirmed measles deaths remained unchanged at 93.
